chain_of_thought: Top native frame is strchr_default, which is a libc helper; first app-level frame below that is decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, called from JNI Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse.

I attempted to search/decompile decodeBytes and the JNI function in Ghidra via MCP, but search_functions_by_name repeatedly returned 404 errors; so I cannot inspect the actual implementation, argument flow, or presence of memcpy/strcpy calls.

Given only the high-level stack and termination reason, I know that strchr_default crashed/aborted, which typically happens on invalid pointer, non-terminated string, or other misuse of C string APIs; however, this could stem from the fuzzer passing arbitrary non-UTF-8 / non-string data via fuzz_one_input into a function that expects a well-formed C string, which may not be reachable with real app inputs.

Without decompiled code, I cannot perform the mandated backward data-flow analysis from the strchr_default caller inside decodeBytes back to JNI and Java, so I cannot prove whether any attacker-controllable length/offset is used without validation. I also lack the Java call graph beyond knowing that JNI bridge Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse exists, and the fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input is clearly non-production.

The presence of a dedicated fuzz harness (fuzz_one_input) strongly suggests this crash occurred under fuzzing using synthetic inputs not representative of normal Java-side usage. Many JNI fuzz harnesses pass raw, unstructured byte arrays into native routines that in production are only ever fed validated or structured XML strings. A strchr-based failure on malformed/null-less data is a common benign fuzzer artifact.

Because I cannot see decodeBytes, I cannot assert an out-of-bounds or use-after-free; all I can infer is an invalid use of strchr. This could be a genuine bug if decodeBytes fails to validate inputs from Java (e.g., assumes null-terminated string but only has a byte buffer and length). However, I cannot confirm that the same unvalidated path exists under real app behavior as opposed to the artificial fuzz_one_input harness.

Given the limited evidence and tool errors, the most reasonable classification is that this is an unclear/non-confirmed vulnerability. There is some possibility of a real bug (unsafe C string ops on attacker-influenced XML data), but lack of code and reachability analysis from real Java entrypoints reduces confidence. I will mark it as not-a-confirmed vulnerability, with medium-low confidence and note that further manual reverse engineering is required.
reasons: Crash occurs in strchr_default called by decodeBytes, indicating misuse of C string routines but not necessarily exploitable memory corruption.
Ghidra MCP search for decodeBytes and the JNI function failed (404), so no code-level evidence of attacker-controlled pointers/lengths or missing bounds checks could be gathered.
The presence of a dedicated fuzz harness (fuzz_one_input) suggests the crash may be triggered only by unrealistic fuzz inputs not reachable in normal app execution.
No Java call-graph or JNI argument validation details are available to establish an untrusted-input-to-crash dataflow path.
recommendations: Manually reverse engineer libtbxml.so, focusing on decodeBytes and its use of strchr/other C string APIs, to determine if unbounded scans or missing length checks exist.
Inspect the Java TBXML.jniParse call sites to understand how inputs are constructed and whether they can contain arbitrary binary data or lack null terminators.

If decodeBytes assumes null-terminated input, add explicit length-based validation/conversion from Java byte arrays or strings before passing to native code, and avoid plain strchr on untrusted buffers.
assumptions: fuzz_one_input is a test-only harness not present in production APK behavior.
The crash reason is related to invalid pointer or unterminated string usage within strchr_default, given typical behavior of that function.
limitations: Unable to decompile or even locate decodeBytes and Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse in Ghidra due to MCP 404 errors, preventing detailed dataflow analysis.
No view of the Java TBXML implementation or real Java call graph, so input controllability and validation cannot be assessed.
No concrete crash context (registers, arguments, exact fault) for strchr_default to distinguish between benign abort and corrupting access.

chain_of_thought: Stack shows strpbrk → decodeBytes → Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse → fuzz_one_input. Termination reported at strpbrk (likely a libc function) but also listed as symbol in libtbxml.so, so either direct call to libc strpbrk or a PLT entry. Root cause is likely misuse of strpbrk (e.g., passing non-null-terminated buffer, invalid pointer, or mismatch between length and buffer).

The first app-level native frame below libc is decodeBytes in libtbxml.so. Without decompiled code, infer common pattern: TBXML is a small XML parser; decodeBytes probably converts a Java byte[]/String into internal representation, using strpbrk to scan for XML delimiters or whitespace. A typical unsafe pattern is using strpbrk on attacker-controlled data without ensuring there is a terminating '\0' within the valid bounds of the buffer provided by JNI/Java, e.g., operating on a raw byte array with embedded NULs or without explicit termination, or using a pointer that can be advanced beyond allocated space due to upstream parsing logic.

JNI bridge Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse is the native entry from TBXML.jniParse. The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input directly feeds arbitrary input into this function (most likely as a byte array or string). Under real app use, this JNI method will be called on XML data coming from untrusted sources (files, network, IPC) – XML parsers are typically fed with such inputs. So attacker-controlled data can flow into decodeBytes and thus into the buffer scanned by strpbrk.

Fuzzer-level conditions (e.g., passing completely invalid or null pointers) are unlikely here because the crash is specifically in strpbrk and not an immediate null deref or harness misuse at JNI boundary. JNI crash signatures for harness misuse often show SEGV on deref of NULL or random address in JNI glue; here, an internal C string function is being exercised on data originating from fuzzed input, indicating the library is attempting to process that data as a C string.

strpbrk normally does not itself crash unless given an invalid pointer or a non-terminated string which causes an out-of-bounds read. On Android, this can manifest as a SIGSEGV or abort when reading beyond the mapped buffer. This is a classic out-of-bounds read due to missing bounds checks or missing null-termination of attacker-controlled data, which falls under memory safety (CWE-125/787 family). Even if only read, it may still be exploitable for information disclosure or cause reliable DoS if parsing arbitrary untrusted XML.

Because TBXML is a generic XML parsing library and used as part of normal app functionality, and the JNI bridge appears to be a thin wrapper that likely forwards a Java string/byte[] and length, the bug is realistically reachable. Fuzzer has demonstrated an input that causes strpbrk to abort; unless we see strong validation (which we cannot due to missing decompile), the conservative and realistic judgement is that decodeBytes fails to enforce C-string invariants before calling strpbrk, making this a genuine library bug rather than a fuzz-only artifact.

However, uncertainty remains: Java TBXML.jniParse might always pass a Java String that is converted via GetStringUTFChars, which returns a null-terminated UTF-8 C string of length determined by the VM, making misuse less likely. But decodeBytes suggests raw byte handling, and fuzz harness name fuzz_one_input implies it may pass arbitrary raw bytes rather than Java-level Strings, increasing risk. Even if production Java code tends to pass properly terminated strings, other consumers (or malformed XML with embedded NULs) might still break assumptions. Given the crash manifests under fuzzing, we mark as likely vulnerability with moderate-to-high confidence, not 1.0.

Severity assessment: Out-of-bounds read via strpbrk on attacker-controlled XML can at minimum crash the app (DoS) and potentially leak memory via side channels, but typical direct exploitation of OOB read in strpbrk to RCE is non-trivial. No evidence of write primitives or control-flow hijack. Thus 'medium' severity for reliable app crash via untrusted XML input. CWE classification: CWE-125 (Out-of-bounds Read) or more general CWE-787; CWE-119 umbrella. Will choose CWE-125 as most precise.

Call sequence: fuzz_one_input → Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse → decodeBytes → strpbrk. JNI bridge TBXML.jniParse is exposed in libtbxml.so. Affected library: libtbxml.so. Recommendations: inspect decodeBytes implementation; replace raw strpbrk with bounded scanning (memchr-style) using known buffer length; ensure buffers derived from Java byte[] are explicitly null-terminated in a separately allocated buffer of length+1; add input validation and tests around NULs and large sizes. Exploit outline: attacker supplies crafted XML via the app's normal XML-consuming interface (network/file), causing TBXML to crash during parsing. Provide generic adb PoC commands using a placeholder Activity or input path as we lack exact names; explain this in assumptions/limitations.
reasons: Crash occurs inside strpbrk called from decodeBytes within libtbxml.so, indicating unsafe C-string handling on processed data
Input path fuzz_one_input → Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse → decodeBytes shows attacker-controlled bytes being parsed as C strings without evident bounds checks or enforced null-termination
strpbrk will only fault if passed invalid or unterminated buffers, implying out-of-bounds read caused by library misuse rather than mere harness misuse
JNI entry is a general-purpose XML parsing function (TBXML), realistically reachable with untrusted XML in production use
recommendations: Review and fix decodeBytes to ensure all buffers passed to strpbrk are derived from null-terminated copies sized as len+1 and explicitly terminated
Replace unbounded strpbrk usage on attacker-controlled data with bounded search functions that take an explicit length and never read past the buffer
Add unit tests and fuzz cases around XML inputs containing embedded NUL bytes, missing terminators, and very long sequences to verify robust parsing
assumptions: decodeBytes receives raw buffers/lengths from JNI without full validation or guaranteed null-termination
The Java TBXML.jniParse method can be invoked on untrusted XML content from network or files in the real application
The observed strpbrk crash is due to out-of-bounds read from an unterminated or otherwise invalid buffer, not due to unrelated libc or harness faults
limitations: Could not decompile libtbxml.so to confirm exact decodeBytes implementation and argument flow
Do not have precise Java-side implementation of TBXML.jniParse or how inputs are constructed in normal app flows
Lack direct crash logs (signal, register state, fault address) to categorically distinguish OOB read from other failure modes

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying crafted XML data that, when passed through TBXML.jniParse, leads decodeBytes to call strpbrk on an invalid or unterminated buffer
prerequisites: Attacker can control or significantly influence XML content parsed via TBXML.jniParse (e.g., via network response, imported file, or IPC)
Application exposes a code path that parses attacker-controlled XML using TBXML
exploit_pipeline: Craft an XML payload with properties (e.g., length, embedded NULs, malformed structure) that stress TBXML's decodeBytes string handling
Deliver this payload to the application via its XML input channel (such as uploading/importing a file or triggering a network fetch that uses TBXML for parsing)
Cause the app to invoke TBXML.jniParse on the crafted XML, leading decodeBytes to call strpbrk on an improperly terminated buffer
strpbrk performs an out-of-bounds read and the process crashes, causing a denial of service and potentially leaking memory information in diagnostics
exploit.notes: Exact Activity, package name, and XML input mechanism are unknown; replace placeholders with real component names once identified.
